summary: McKenzie reports that Google AdWords unexpectedly doubled minimum bid prices on many of his keywords, destroying the profitability he had carefully optimized over weeks. After finally achieving a profitable cost-per-acquisition of 30 cents per download, his CPA crept back up to 37 cents due to Google's quality penalty raising minimum bids from 7-9 cents to 15 cents. He finds this particularly frustrating because some of the penalized keywords had strong click-through rates and highly relevant landing pages. He suspects either large portfolio bidders like eBay raised prices across the board, or Google applied a low-quality penalty incorrectly.
key_points: Google silently raised minimum bids on half his vocabulary campaign keywords from 7-9 cents to 15 cents, erasing weeks of optimization work
Even keywords with 6% CTR and highly relevant, hand-crafted landing pages were hit with the increased minimum bids
His only AdWords competition comes from companies bidding on massive keyword portfolios numbering in the millions, not direct competitors
The new CPC rates may make it mathematically impossible to run the campaign profitably
money_quotes: After finally achieving 30 cents per download (where AdWords is actually profitable) and keeping it there for a week my CPA creeped up again to 37 cents.
It turns out that Google turned off half of my words in the vocabulary campaign, requiring a minimum bid of 15 cents for what used to cost 7-9.

I got my cost doubled on "Dolch sight word bingo", of all things, which is a) 6% CTR on a bad day and b) my landing page for that is human-generated and about as responsive to the query as you can possibly get.
My only competition for AdWords is people bidding on gigantic portfolios, ranging probably in the millions of search strings.
I've sunk dozens of hours into optimizing that campaign to get it where it is, and I don't think its even mathematically possible to make it have positive returns at the new-and-improved CPC rate.
key_insight: Platform dependency creates existential risk for small businesses when algorithmic pricing changes can instantly destroy carefully optimized unit economics with no recourse.
